steps.title: Identify what the data must show
steps.content: The assertion is that Drosophila collected in cooler months should have lower reproductive fitness. Wing centroid size is the proxy for body size, which correlates with fitness. To support the assertion, the data must show that a cooler month produced smaller wing centroid size than a warmer month — specifically for the same sex, so the comparison is clean.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'average male wing centroid size was 1.98 mm in September but 2.31 mm in July': This does show a cooler-month decrease, but September (cooler) yields smaller male wings than July (warmer) — wait, 1.98 < 2.31, so this actually supports the claim directionally. However, the rationale shows the table supports D specifically for females; A's male values do not match the actual table data provided. ✗
  • Choice B — 'average male wing centroid size was consistently smaller than average female wing centroid size in all four months': This compares the two sexes within the same month — it says nothing about seasonal variation or cooler vs. warmer months. It cannot support a seasonal fitness claim. ✗
  • Choice C — 'average monthly low temperature was lower in May than in September': Temperature data alone, with no wing size measurement, cannot connect to reproductive fitness or body size. It omits the proxy measure entirely. ✗
  • Choice D — 'average female wing centroid size was smaller in September than in July': September is cooler than July; smaller wing centroid size means smaller body size, which means lower reproductive fitness. This directly illustrates the predicted relationship for female flies. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
